title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Rosette in bottom left and right corners. Two nail holes at top, one hole at bottom right; remains of iron nail in hole at bottom left. Depicts Oba with mudfish legs, facing front, holding a leopard by tail in each hand. Oba wears cylindrical beaded crown with oro protrusion and three ornamental beads, deep beaded collar, tunic and patterned skirt with series of pendant masks depicting animal heads. Two mudfish suspended from belt around waist. Leopards shown in profile wearing collars with bells attached.;
curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group; Oba sacrificing leopards is ritual act performed at coronation and at Igue, the annual rites dedicated to strengthening his mystical powers.; Part of Processional Pillar Set 2. Row 2A. Oba with mudfish from belt, swinging leopards (Gunsch, 2018).; Read & Dalton 1899: This figure represents the king in the same character [as Af1898,0115.30], but with bead ornaments fringed with bells upon his dress. His legs are here shown, and the fish issue from his sides. In each hand he is swinging a leopard as before, the leopards wearing collars with small hawk-bells, which would suggest that they are tame. On each wrist is a broad bronze or ivory armlet. In the lower corners of the panel are raised rosettes.
acquisition_notes: Following the British occupation of Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory and wood were looted by British forces from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. During the summer of 1897 the Crown Agents for the Colonies, on behalf of the Foreign Office, agreed a temporary loan of 304 plaques to the British Museum. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. The Museum initially received 203 of these plaques as a gift from the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. In the summer of 1898 a further eleven plaques were sent to the British Museum from the Foreign Office and three of these were selected by the Museum and were subsequently presented as a gift. Of the remaining plaques the Foreign Office retained eight and the rest were offered for sale to major museums, collectors and private dealers in Europe and the UK. Today over nine hundred plaques are known to exist in museums and private collections around the world. See Collection File: Af1898,0115.1-203 (previously Eth.Doc.185).
expedition_note: This plaque was looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top centre, two holes at bottom right and left. Depicts three standing figures, possibly leopard-hunters, facing front. Central figure holds human-headed rattle staff in left hand and circular dish in right hand. Figure on his right has leopard skin attached to strap across chest. Figure on left plays two cylinder drums held under left arm. All figures have tiered hairstyles. Wear necklaces, armlets or bracelets, sashes tied over left hips, and leopard skin wrap-around skirts.;

Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group; Read & Dalton 1899: Three musicians with loin-cloths of leopard skin. The figure on the left has a leopard's skin suspended from his neck. The central figure has his right hand on a shallow bowl, while in his left is an ivory staff with a rattle at the upper end surmounted by a human head (Cf. Af1898,0630.1). The man on the two cylinders, slung over his left shoulder (Cf. Af1898,0115.129).

description: Relief plaque, fragment; lost-wax cast in brass. Depicts leopard's skull, seen from above.;

acquisition_notes: Af1928,1006.6 to 9 are from Africa, and come from a larger group of miscellaneous objects from other parts of the world given by Dr W L Hildburgh. No.6 is thought to come from Benin. During the British Expedition to Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory, coral and wood were looted by British soldiers from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. See Collection File: Af1928,1006.6

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, almost square in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Single rosette in low relief in top right corner. One nail hole at top centre; one hole at midway on right side, two at bottom right and left. Depicts central standing warrior figure holding eben sword aloft in right hand, left hand on belt. Wears tall helmet with side panels and beads, deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, quadrangular bell on chest, long-sleeved feather tunic and wrap-around skirt, armlets and beaded anklets. Has dagger and animal-headed hip ornament on left side. Flanked by two smaller scale musicians; on left holding two gongs, on right blowing a horn held in both hands. Musicians have side locks on left and wear net-type caps with feather on left side, beaded necklaces, leopard's tooth necklaces, and patterned wrap-around skirts. Musician on left wears colied bracelets, figure on right wears armlets.;
curators_comments: Cental figure possibly represents vassal warlord.
acquisition_notes: During the British Expedition to Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory, coral and wood were looted by British soldiers from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Five rosettes in low relief; three along one side and two along other. Two nail holes at one end at right and left, two holes at midway on right and left sides, one hole at other end. Depicts snake, shown overhead, in relief. Snake has pointed mouth, large eyes, sinuous body narrowing to tail. Skin represented by circular patterns inset with four small circles.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at one end, one hole at other end. Depicts four ?mudfish in relief, facing left, in profile. Mudfish have ringed eyes, open mouths holding circular objects, dorsal fins, two gill rings, two pectoral fins, and tri-lobed tails. Heads and bodies decorated with single dot crosshatching.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two rosettes in relief in top corners. Two nail holes at right and left corners at both ends. Depicts seated leopard with upper body shown from above, lower body in profile. Leopard has whiskers and circular upright ears. Skin represented by plain circles surrounded by stippling. Back legs shown one in front of other.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, horzontal; rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at bottom of plaque. Depicts single ?mudfish, in right profile. Fish has ringed eye, slightly open mouth, single gill ring, ?two pectoral fins, and forked tail. Head decorated with crosshatching, body with dotted crosshatching.;

acquisition_notes: During the British Expedition to Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory, coral and wood were looted by British soldiers from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. Others seem to have circulated in the years after the Expedition within Nigeria and were subsequently acquired by British soldiers or colonial officials employed or resident there. The donor of this plaque was employed by the Forestry Department, Benin Province, Southern Nigeria and was based in Benin City from 1903. See Collection File: Af1908,1205.1-10.
expedition_note: This plaque was probably looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. One nail hole at top right; one partial hole at bottom right. Depicts single standing emada figure, facing front, holding circular feathered fan in right hand. Figure has tiered hairstyle with side plaits terminating in beads. Naked, except for deep beaded collar, two sets of coiled bracelets on each arm, waist beads, and elaborate anklets or leg decoration.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. One nail hole at top right (tail end); two holes midway at right and left; two holes at bottom right and left. Depicts snake, from above, eating mudfish. Snake has sinuous body decorated with circular motifs with three inset circles. Elongated oval eyes on top. Mudfish head in profile; two gill rings, two pectoral fins, ringed eye and slightly open mouth. Head decorated with single dot cross-hatching.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Three nail holes at one end at right, left and centre; two holes at other end at right and left. Depicts four mudfish in high relief, viewed overhead, facing in same direction. Mudfish have curved barbels, prominent circular eyes, dorsal ridges, three gill rings, two pectoral fins, and tri-lobed tail. Heads decorated with crosshatching, bodies with overlapping scales.;

description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Two nail holes at top right and left corners; two at bottom right and left corners. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Depicts eben sword, blade facing downwards, in low relief. Large leaf-shaped blade with central rib decorated with dot patterns and twisted ring handle.;
curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group
acquisition_notes: Following the British occupation of Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory and wood were looted by British forces from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. During the summer of 1897 the Crown Agents for the Colonies, on behalf of the Foreign Office, agreed a temporary loan of 304 plaques to the British Museum. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. The Museum initially received 203 of these plaques as a gift from the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. In the summer of 1898 a further eleven plaques were sent to the British Museum from the Foreign Office and three of these were selected by the Museum and were subsequently presented as a gift. Of the remaining plaques the Foreign Office retained eight and the rest were offered for sale to major museums, collectors and private dealers in Europe and the UK. Today over nine hundred plaques are known to exist in museums and private collections around the world. See Collection File: Af1898,0115.1-203 (previously Eth.Doc.185).
expedition_note: This plaque was looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Partial nail hole at bottom left. Depicts two coiled mudfish, facing downwards, seen from above. Mudfish have large barbels, ringed eyes, dorsal fins, two gill rings, one pectoral fin, and forked tails. Heads decorated with single dot crosshatching, body with angled lines.;

acquisition_notes: During the British Expedition to Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory, coral and wood were looted by British soldiers from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. Others seem to have circulated in the years after the Expedition within Nigeria and were subsequently acquired by British soldiers or colonial officials employed or resident there. The donor of this plaque was employed by the Forestry Department, Benin Province, Southern Nigeria and was based in Benin City from 1903. See Collection File: Af1908,1205.1-10.
expedition_note: This plaque was probably looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top right and left corners; two holes along right and left sides; three holes at bottom left, right and centre. Depicts single standing figure, possibly priest?, facing front, holding sword under left arm. Figure has centrally parted hairstyle, with long tresses, some plaited. Wears beaded necklace, leopard's skin baldric with pendent terminating in bell, and with larger bell attached, bracelets, armlet on upper right arm, sash tied over left hip and wrap-around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes in corners at right side. Depicts leopard in profile, biting head of ? goat with rope around neck. Leopard has whiskers, upright ear and curling tail over back. Front paw on goat's back, both back legs shown, one in front of other. Leopard's skin represented by plain circles surrounded by stippling. Goat has long snout, pointed ears, short legs and skin represented by stippling.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two down-turned crescents in low relief at top and bottom, right and left; two rosettes in low relief at midway right and left. Three nail holes at top right, left and centre. Three holes at bottom right, left and centre. Depicts single standing figure, facing front. Figure has tiered hairstyle with plaited and coiled lock at left side and feather above. Wears leopard's tooth necklace, armlet on upper right arm, multiple bracelets on both arms, mu;ltiple anklets/leglets, fringed baldric across chest, beaded sash tied over left hip, and fringed wrap-around skirt.;
curators_comments: Read & Dalton 1899: Single figure unarmed, the hair dressed like the principal figures in Af1898,0115.46. He wears a leopard's tooth collar; a fringed baldric, with bell-shaped projections, armlets and anklets. His loin-cloth has an extended end. In the field are four reversed crescents and two rosettes.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top left and centre. Three (?) holes at bottom right, left and centre. Depicts two standing courtier figures, facing front, holding double gongs in left hands and strikers in right hands. Wear helmets with stiff front panels, horsehair behind and two feathers at top, leopard's tooth necklaces, quadrangular bells on bands across chest, and armlets. Dresssed in decorative chest bands, short-sleeved under-garments, leopard-faced body armour with pendants terminating in bells, wrap-around patterned skirts.;

acquisition_notes: Following the British occupation of Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory and wood were looted by British forces from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. One nail hole at top, two holes at bottom. Depicts standing figure of Oba (possibly Esigie), facing front, flanked by two standing warrior figures holding shields above his head. Oba wears beaded crown with feather at left, deep beaded collar, multiple beaded necklaces, beaded bracelets and anklets, and patterned wrap-around skirt tied with sash on left hip. Holds ceremonial switch in right hand and places left hand on top of horn held by small attendant at side. Two warriors have tiered hairstyle with single feather and coiled side lock at left side. Wear armlets, leopard's tooth necklace, patterned wrap-around skirts with sash tied over left hip. Both carry swords on left side. Smaller naked emada figures stand on either side of Oba. Figure on right has plaited hairstyle parted at front, wears patterned hip ornament tied on left hip, beaded necklace and bracelets. Carries ekpokin box in left hand. Figure on left has crested hairstyle, wears necklace and bracelets. Carries sword at front of body supported by left hand and holds horn in right hand.;
curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group; Direct pair with BM plaque Af1898,0115.38. Oba carries flexible switch, uwenrhien otan, in preparation for sacrificial ceremony. His retainers shield him from the sun, and also from hostile spiritual forces.; Plankensteiner 2007: These two plaques [with Af1898,0115.38] simultaneously arrived at the British Museum in 1898. Though a little different in size, iconographically and stylistically they are nearly identical. The corresponding features make it all the more likely that they were meant to be a pair. As is known about the old palace buildings at the time of these plaques' manufacturing, this sort of plaque was attached - and demonstrably in symmetrical ordering - onto the pillars of the verandas and entrances. The opulent jewelry of the central figure, identified as Oba Esigie by Barbara Blackmun (1997) based on a comparisson of depictions on carved ivories and plaques, comprises of a high beaded choker, a multirow necklace of agate and coral beads, large agate pendants hanging on the sides of the coral beaded cap, adorned with a feather, arm and foot rings, a precious wrapper, presumably made from imported Indian or Portuguese fabrics, and a luxurious belt-and hip decoration with a mask. In his right hand the king holds a ceremonial switch. His left hand rests on the hand of one of the two small and - except for some jewelry - naked attendants, who also carries a sword. The small attendant figure to the left of the plaque carries a spool-shaped 'ekpokin' box. The two larger figures wear precious clothes. They protect the Oba's head from both sides with raised shields. All attendant figures have an unusual hairdo: a hair lock adorns the left ear of the two shield carrying figures, the hairstyle of both small attendants are reminiscent of those worn by the Ooton priests and the hair crest of the Oduduwa masks. The depicted scene presumably represents a ritual at a palace ceremony, the Igue, for instance, or the Ugie Erha Oba.; Read & Dalton 1899: Plaque made of bronze: A Chief with two attendants, and between them two smaller figures... With a bead cap like that of Af1898,0115.15, and holds a wand in the right hand, while the left is supported by a naked boy who holds his sword. The attendants, who are clothed like the figure to the left in Af1898,0115,16 but have the hair dressed in overlapping layers, with a feather and a spiral curl over the left ear, are holding a shield over the chief's head. The second small figure holds in his left hand a small cylindrical drum (compare Af1898,0115,131). All the figures are marked with tribal cicatrices.

description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two large rosettes in low relief at top and bottom, right and left. Two nail holes at top right and left; two holes at bottom right and left. Depicts single standing figure, facing front. Figure has tiered hairstyle and central circular mark on forehead. Wears two beaded necklaces, armlets, sash across chest, beaded anklets and patterned wrap-around skirt.;
curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group
acquisition_notes: Following the British occupation of Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory and wood were looted by British forces from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. During the summer of 1897 the Crown Agents for the Colonies, on behalf of the Foreign Office, agreed a temporary loan of 304 plaques to the British Museum. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. The Museum initially received 203 of these plaques as a gift from the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. In the summer of 1898 a further eleven plaques were sent to the British Museum from the Foreign Office and three of these were selected by the Museum and were subsequently presented as a gift. Of the remaining plaques the Foreign Office retained eight and the rest were offered for sale to major museums, collectors and private dealers in Europe and the UK. Today over nine hundred plaques are known to exist in museums and private collections around the world. See Collection File: Af1898,0115.1-203 (previously Eth.Doc.185).
expedition_note: This plaque was looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top right and left corners, one nail in situ at bottom right. Depicts single standing emada figure, facing front, holding unidentified circular object in left hand. Figure has tiered hairstyle, plaits terminating in beads on either side of face, triangular mark down nose. Figure is naked except for beaded collar, beaded necklace, armlets, and beaded anklets.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Four nail holes at top; three holes at bottom. Depicts two standing figures, facing front, each holding flexible switch in right hand; left hand clenched with thumb extended. Three vertical marks above each eye. Each wears basketry cap with oro protrusion, deep beaded collar, coral bead under-garments, crossed beaded baldric, bracelet, beaded anklet and wrap-around skirt with sash tied on left hip.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. One nail hole at top centre, one partial hole at top right.Two holes at midway right and left. One hole at bottom centre. Depicts three standing courtier figures, facing front. Central figure and figure on left hold leopard-skin bags in both hands. Both have plaits on either side of face terminating in beads. Wear beaded caps, deep beaded collars, multiple beaded necklaces, armlets, beaded anklets, fringed wrap-around skirts. Figure on right has right arm outstretched, hand missing. Has plaits on either side of face terminating in beads. Wears beaded cap, deep beaded collar, multiple beaded necklaces with larger beaded necklace on outside, armlets, beaded anklets, and fringed wrap-around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling.Two nail holes at each end end at right and left. Depicts two intertwined ?mudfish in relief, viewed overhead, facing in opposite directions. Mudfish have large, wide barbels, prominent circular eyes, dorsal ridges, two gill rings, single pectoral fin, and forked tails. Heads decorated with single dot crosshatching, bodies with angled lines, alternating between dotted and plain surfaces.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Three nail holes at top. Depicts standing Oba, facing front, flanked by two standing enobore, also facing front, supporting his arms. Oba wears cylindrical beaded crown with oro protrusion, deep beaded collar, crossed beaded bands on chest, armlets and beaded anklets, and patterned skirt with series of pendant masks depicting crocodile heads suspended from belt. Supporters similarly dressed: tiered hairstyle with oro protrusion, beaded necklaces, armlets and wrap-around skirts tied over left hip.;

description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Depicts central standing warrior figure holding eben sword aloft in right hand. Sword under left arm and plain bell at left side. Figure has short plaits terminating in beads at either side of face. Wears helmet with stiff front panel with central image of eben sword and animal skin at back, feather in left side; beaded necklace, leopard's tooth necklace, chest band with quadrangular bell attached, short-sleeved under-garment, armlets and wrap-around skirt. Both musicians wear patterned wrap-around skirts, bracelets, and have baldrics across chest with swords attached at left sides.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at each end end at right and left. Depicts five mudfish in relief, viewed overhead, facing in same direction. Outer four mudfish have curving bodies, central mudfish has straight body. Mudfish have curved barbels, prominent circular eyes, dorsal ridges, two pectoral fins, and tri-lobed tail. Two mudfish at back have two gill rings, other three mudfish have three gill rings. Heads decorated with single dot crosshatching, bodies with dotted lozenges.;

However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. During the summer of 1897 the Crown Agents for the Colonies, on behalf of the Foreign Office, agreed a temporary loan of 304 plaques to the British Museum. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. The Museum initially received 203 of these plaques as a gift from the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. In the summer of 1898 a further eleven plaques were sent to the British Museum from the Foreign Office and three of these were selected by the Museum and were subsequently presented as a gift. Of the remaining plaques the Foreign Office retained eight and the rest were offered for sale to major museums, collectors and private dealers in Europe and the UK. Today over nine hundred plaques are known to exist in museums and private collections around the world. See Collection File: Af1898,0115.1-203 (previously Eth.Doc.185).

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Three nail holes at top; three holes at bottom. Depicts two standing figures, facing forwards. Warrior figure on left holds eben sword in left hand. Plaits terminating in beads at either side of face. Wears tall textured helmet, deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, quadrangular bell on chest, bracelets and anklets. Dressed in leopard-faced body armour with pendant bells, chest band, and patterned wrap-around skirt. Figure on right holds leopard skin-covered ekpokin container in both hands. Has tiered hairstyle with plaits terminating in beads at either side of face. Wears beaded udaeba band across forehead, deep beaded collar, multi-strand necklace, beaded bracelets and anklets, Dressed in patterned wrap-around skirt and sash tied on left hip.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top right and centre, possible damaged hole at top left. Remains of iron nail at bottom left. Depicts central prone animal lying on side with two figures of seated leopards biting the animal's head and hind quarters. Leopards shown in high relief from above. Two further larger scale leopards in relief, at either side, depicted in profile. All leopards shown with dotted circle marking and upright ears. Larger leopards shown with bared teeth and whiskers.;
curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group
acquisition_notes: Following the British occupation of Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory and wood were looted by British forces from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. During the summer of 1897 the Crown Agents for the Colonies, on behalf of the Foreign Office, agreed a temporary loan of 304 plaques to the British Museum. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. The Museum initially received 203 of these plaques as a gift from the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. In the summer of 1898 a further eleven plaques were sent to the British Museum from the Foreign Office and three of these were selected by the Museum and were subsequently presented as a gift. Of the remaining plaques the Foreign Office retained eight and the rest were offered for sale to major museums, collectors and private dealers in Europe and the UK. Today over nine hundred plaques are known to exist in museums and private collections around the world. See Collection File: Af1898,0115.1-203 (previously Eth.Doc.185).
expedition_note: This plaque was looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at one end, one hole and partial hole at other end. Depicts two intertwined ?mudfish in relief, viewed overhead, facing in opposite directions. Mudfish have wide, curving barbels, prominent circular eyes, decorated dorsal ridges, three gill rings, single pectoral fin, and tri-lobed tails. Heads decorated with single dot crosshatching, bodies with dotted interlocking lozenges.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Six crescents in low relief at top right and left, midway right and left, and bottom right and left. One nail hole at top left; two holes at bottom right and left. Depicts heads of two trussed cows (?) or antelopes (?) in relief, seen from above. Animals have in-curving horns and erect ears; skin represented with stippling.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Partial nail hole at bottom right. Depicts central standing figure, facing forward, holidng eben sword aloft in right hand, left hand on belt. Body scarification on upper arms and torso. Wears bell-shaped beaded headgear with finial knob and feather at left side, deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, beaded sash across chest, armlets, beaded anklets, patterned wrap-around skirt and leopard figure on left hip. Smaller scale figure on right with tiered hairstyle and long plaits at either side of head. Figure is naked except for beaded collar and anklets. Holds kola nut container in both hands.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Three (?) nail holes along top right and centre, partial hole at top left. Depicts three standing figures, facing front, each holding vessel in both hands. Figure on right holds handled cup with projections, central figure holds shallow bowl, figure on left holds small handled jug. Figures have tiered hairstyles with coils of plaited hair on either side of heads. Wear short beaded collars, two beaded necklaces, armlets, anklets, and patterned wrap-around skirts.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Four (?) nail holes at top, four holes at bottom. Depicts courtier on horseback, facing front, holding on to the hands of two attendants. Tiered hairstyle with beaded forehead band and feather at left side. Wears deep beaded collar, armlets, beaded anklets, and patterned wrap-around skirt. Sits side saddle on horse. Flanked by two attendants, facing front, with distinctive crested hairstyles and long plaits on either side of face. Wear beaded necklaces, bracelets, baldrics diagonally across chest with swords on left hips and patterned wrap-around skirts. Horse depicted facing forwards. Smaller scale page figure in bottom left corner holds rope attached to horse's bridle. Crested hairstyle with side plaits, beaded necklace, baldric across chest with sword on left side and patterned wrap-around skirt.;

He wears a loin-cloth, with prominent extended end and bead girdle, bead collar and frontlet, and broad armlets. The attendants are simply dressed, with partly shaven heads, with crests and side patches, and shoulder belts supporting swords. A third smaller figure leads the horse by a single rein, as in Af1898.0115.44.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top right and centre; partial hole at top left; two holes midway at left and right; two holes at bottom left and centre. Depicts single standing figure of the Ehioba, facing front, holding a forked switch aloft in right hand. Has partially shaved head with tiered hairstyle on either side, central lock at front and curled side plait at left. Triangular mark down nose. Oro protrusion attached to top of head. Wears deep beaded collar, full-length, long-sleeved patterned gown, and bracelets.;

Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries.; Harry Beasley purchased this plaque from Åke Sjögren, Stockholm. Archives in the Ethnographic Museum, Stockholm record that this plaque (and plaque Af1944,04.9) were bought by Sjögren on 11(?) January 1899 from the London-based dealer William Webster for £15 each. The Beasley Collection ledger entry for 13 July 1928 reads: 'Benin. A Bronze Plaque, of a single figure carrying a double pointed spear? + dressed in a long one piece garment decorated with a punched and incised design. Wearing a coral choker + curious pointed head dress. 18" long. 8 3/4" wide. Bt A. Sjogren. Hofslagangatan, Stockholm. Carriage + packing included.'; Ehioba is leader of the Ooton guild, selected from descendants of previous rulers of Benin, who served the high priest Osuan. The bulge beneath his tunic on his chest represents the concealed jawbones of adeceased Iyase (political opponent to Oba).; See Collection File: Af1944,04.1-77.
acquisition_notes: During the British Expedition to Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory, coral and wood were looted by British soldiers from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. This plaque was previously in the collection of the Cranmore Ethnographical Museum established by Harry Geoffrey and Irene Marguerite Beasley in 1928. The Beasley ledgers record its purchase on 13 July 1928 from Åke Sjögren, Stockholm. Archives in the Ethnographic Museum, Stockholm record that this plaque (and plaque Af1944,04.9) were bought by Sjögren on 11(?) January 1899 from the London-based dealer William Webster for £15 each. The entire Beasley collection was distributed following Harry Beasley's death in 1939. This plaque formed part of the major donation made by Irene Beasley to the British Museum in 1944 (for the full gift see Af1944,04.1-337).
expedition_note: This plaque was looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Three nail holes at top right, left and centre. Three holes at bottom right, left and centre. Depicts two standing figures, facing front, holding flexible switches in right hands; sheathed swords at their left sides. Figures have plaits terminating in beads at both sides of faces. Wear brimmed helmets, beaded necklaces, armlets, baldrics across chest, beaded anklets, sashes tied over left hip and patterned wrap-around skirts.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Two leopard heads in low relief in top left and right corners; two crocodile heads in low relief in bottom left and right corners. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Depicts single standing courtier figure, facing front, in high relief. Holding circular rattle in both outstretched hands. Wears crested beaded cap with multiple strings of beads at sides of face, deep beaded collar, beaded sash across chest, armlets, beaded anklets, patterned sash tied over left hip and patterned wrap-around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top right and left corners. Two holes at bottom right and centre. Depicts standing warrior figure, facing front, holding eben sword aloft in right hand, left hand holding sword under arm. Figure has hair plait curling from under helmet on left side. Wears textured helmet, feather in left side, beaded necklace, leopard's tooth necklace, baldric with pendent panels across chest, bracelets, human-headed hip ornament on left side, and patterned wrap-around skirt. Smaller scale hornblower at left side. Has centrally shaved head with middle groove, hair plaits terminating in beads at both sides of face. Wears beaded necklace, bracelets, and short patterned wrap-around skirt.;

In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group; See plaques Af1898,0115.75 and 77 where central figure carries similar crocodile-headed staff. Believed to represent trader.; Read & Dalton 1899: Single figure, dressed in the style of the central person in Af1898,0115.77, and holding a similar cloth. At the corners are four rosettes.
acquisition_notes: Following the British occupation of Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory and wood were looted by British forces from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. During the summer of 1897 the Crown Agents for the Colonies, on behalf of the Foreign Office, agreed a temporary loan of 304 plaques to the British Museum. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. The Museum initially received 203 of these plaques as a gift from the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. In the summer of 1898 a further eleven plaques were sent to the British Museum from the Foreign Office and three of these were selected by the Museum and were subsequently presented as a gift. Of the remaining plaques the Foreign Office retained eight and the rest were offered for sale to major museums, collectors and private dealers in Europe and the UK. Today over nine hundred plaques are known to exist in museums and private collections around the world. See Collection File: Af1898,0115.1-203 (previously Eth.Doc.185).
expedition_note: This plaque was looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges (largely missing). Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling and at right side is stylised version of Portuguese head. One nail hole at top right and two holes at either side at bottom. Crocodile head in relief in top right corner. Depicts European (Portuguese) figure with long hair and beard holding a staff with tiered quadrangular head in right hand; cross-guard sword in scabbard on left hip attached to baldric across chest. Figure wears helmet with bosses on top; feather at one side. Dressed in short-sleeved tunic with buttons at front, long-sleeved patterned tunic below. Knee-length pleated patterned 'skirt'; long decorated breeches below.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges (largely missing). Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two rosettes in relief in top left and right corners. One nail hole at top right and remains of iron nail at top left, two nail holes at centre right and left. Depicts central standing figure, facing front, holding eben sword aloft in right hand with left hand on belt. Wears bell-shaped beaded headgear with finial knob and feather at left side, deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, beaded sash across chest, armlets and patterned wrap-around skirt. Two smaller scale flanking figures, both with tiered hairstyle and feather on left side. Figure on right blows horn, figure on left holds pair of gongs or bells.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at top right and left, two holes at bottom right and left. Depicts single standing bearded musician, facing front. Holds stringed instrument upright in both hands. Has coiled side plait on left side. Wears helmet with cowrie shells and T-shaped frontpiece; central feather from top of helmet. Figure wears coiled neckring, beaded necklace, leopard's tooth necklace, short-sleeved garment, animal skin chest band and body armour, coiled bracelets, sash tied over left hip, and short fringed wrap- around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Leopard heads in low relief in bottom right and left corners. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two nail holes at centre top; two holes at bottom left and centre. Depicts central standing warrior figure, facing front, holding eben sword aloft in right hand and barbed spear in left hand with sword below. Wears tall conical headgear, deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, quadrangular bell on chest, bracelets and beaded anklets. Dressed in short-sleeved patterned tunic, leopard-faced body armour and patterned wrap-around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectnagular in form with side flanges (largely missing). Holes for attachment at top and bottom right. Background surface is decorated with stylised quatrefoil (river leaf) patterns and stippling. Depicts standing figure, facing front, holding container in form of cow's or antelope's head, in both hands. Figure has hair locks parted down centre gathered into two bunches at front. title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Head and torso of four European (Portuguese) figures in each corner, all in profile facing inwards. Figures have long hair; wear brimmed helmets with bosses and feather at top, buttoned jerkins and patterned long-sleeved under-garment. All hold unidentified object to mouths. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Three nail holes at top right, left and centre. Depicts single standing warrior figure or sword-bearer, facing front, holding sword in left hand. Figure has centrally parted tiered hairstyle with long beaded plait at left side. Lower legs painted with lozenge and circle motifs. Elongated trianguar mark along nose. Wears beaded necklace and choker, armlet and bracelet on right arm, tasselled baldric across chest with large ridged bell attached at left side, elaborate loincloth.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two crocodile heads at top and two crocodile heads at bottom, flanking Oba. Two nail holes at top, one hole at bottom. Depicts standing Oba, facing front, flanked by two standing enobore, also facing front, supporting his arms. Oba wears cylindrical beaded crown with oro protrusion, deep beaded collar, crossed beaded bands on chest, armlets and beaded anklets, and patterned skirt with series of pendant masks depicting crocodile heads suspended from belt. Supporters similarly dressed: tiered hairstyle with oro protrusion, beaded necklaces, armlets and wrap-around skirts tied over left hip.;

curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group; Blurton, 1997 Brass was intimately associated with kingship in the Benin empire and plaques, cast by the lost-wax process, were used to cover the wooden beams that supported the roof in the royal palace, the centre of religious activities that controlled the well-being of the entire empire. Those seen here show scenes of court life and ritual which involve rulers, warriors and officials. Naked figures are royal pages, their nudity contrasting with the splendid cloth and coral-bead costumes of royalty. Depictions of the European adventurers and traders, who sold the metal from which the plaques were cast, also appear and local interest centred on their long hair, hooked noses, dress and weapons. Since they were seen as messengers of the god Olokun, a white-faced deity who sends wealth and children over the sea, their presence in the palace is probably less a celebration of their own wealth and power than those of the Oba (king), who is said to have defeated Olokun in battle, stripping him of his riches and finery.

description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. See Collection File: Af1897,0619,1.
expedition_note: This plaque was looted from Benin City in 1897. The donor of the plaque served as Deputy Chief of Staff on the British Expedition to Benin City.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Rosettes in low relief in top left and two bottom corners. One nail hole at top right, three further holes along right side, two holes at botttom right and left corners. Depicts single standing emada figure, facing front. Holds animal skin-covered ekpokin container in left hand, with container's strap in right hand. Figure has tiered hairstyle with shaved central section and two coiled plaits at either side of face. Wears necklace, bracelet, loincloth tied over left hip and circular object with horsehair on right side.;
curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group
acquisition_notes: Following the British occupation of Benin City (Edo) in 1897 objects made of brass, ivory and wood were looted by British forces from the royal palace, its storerooms and compounds. Some of these objects were sold or exchanged on the coast. However, many were brought to the UK where they were sold through private auction, donated to museums, or retained by soldiers of the expedition. The British Museum successfully petitioned the government to secure some of the relief plaques and over 300 were sent to the UK by the Consul-General [Sir] Ralph Moor and placed at the Foreign Office. During the summer of 1897 the Crown Agents for the Colonies, on behalf of the Foreign Office, agreed a temporary loan of 304 plaques to the British Museum. In September these were placed on public display in the Assyrian basement where they attracted considerable public attention. The Museum initially received 203 of these plaques as a gift from the Secretary of State for Foreign Affairs. In the summer of 1898 a further eleven plaques were sent to the British Museum from the Foreign Office and three of these were selected by the Museum and were subsequently presented as a gift. Of the remaining plaques the Foreign Office retained eight and the rest were offered for sale to major museums, collectors and private dealers in Europe and the UK. Today over nine hundred plaques are known to exist in museums and private collections around the world. See Collection File: Af1898,0115.1-203 (previously Eth.Doc.185).
expedition_note: This plaque was looted during the British Expedition to Benin City in 1897.

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Rosettes in low relief in all four corners. Two nail holes at top right and left corners, one hole at midway on right side, two holes at bottom right and left. Depicts single standing courtier figure, facing front, holding eben sword aloft in right hand and shield in left hand. Wears beaded cap with feather on left side, deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, beaded sash across chest, beaded bracelets and anklets, belt tied on left hip with leopard face hip ornament and patterned wrap-around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Wears ? animal skin helmet with tall protrusion and chin strap, deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, under-tunic, quadrangular bell, beaded bracelets, body armour, patterned wrap-around skirt and beaded anklets.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Two rosettes in low relief at top right and left corners, one rosette at midway on right side; one rosette at bottom right corner. Three nail holes at top; two square holes midway at right and left; three holes at bottom. Depicts central standing figure, facing front, holding staff terminating in crocodile head with mudfish in mouth in right hand. Holds left hand of emada figure with left hand. Figure has double plaits terminating in beads at either side of face. Wears helmet decorated with snakes with side flaps, deep collar, beaded necklace and anklets. Dressed in wrap-around skirt and sash tied over left hip. Smaller scale naked emade figure on left has centrally parted tiered hairstyle with side plaits. Holds ? whip folded in left hand.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Four nail holes at top, four holes at bottom. Depicts standing figure of Oba (possibly Esigie), facing front, flanked by two standing warrior figures holding shields above his head. Oba wears beaded crown with feather at left, deep beaded collar, multiple beaded necklaces, beaded bracelets and anklets, and patterned wrap-around skirt tied with sash on left hip. Holds ceremonial switch in right hand and places left hand on top of horn held by small attendant at side. Two warriors have tiered hairstyle with single feather and coiled side lock at left side. Wear armlets, leopard's tooth necklace, patterned wrap-around skirts with sash tied over left hip. Both carry swords on left side. Smaller naked emada figures stand on either side of Oba. Figure on right has tiered hairstyle with shaven central section, wears patterned hip ornament tied on left hip and beaded necklace. Carries ekpokin box in left hand. Figure on left has crested haristyle and wears two beaded necklaces. Carries sword in left hand at front of body and holds horn in right hand.;
curators_comments: The relief brass plaques that used to decorate the Oba's (king's) palace are among the most well-known of all the royal arts of Benin. Although frequently described as 'Benin Bronzes' most plaques are made of leaded brass in various compositions. It is widely accepted that they date to the 16th-17th centuries. In the years prior to the British Expedition royal influence in Benin was increasingly under threat from rival powers, both internal and external, with a focus on economic power and control of the important trading monopolies. However, the court and palace remained the political and spiritual centre of the Benin Kingdom. Earlier accounts written by Europeans visiting the city describe its size and scale. The palace complex was set up around atrium courtyards; some had galleries with wooden pillars supporting the roof. Brass plaques, probably made in matching pairs, were fixed to these pillars. The Benin brass plaques represent a distinct and unique corpus of work, unparalleled elsewhere on the continent. They are cast using the cire perdue (lost wax) technique and show significant variation in the depth of the relief. Some of the plaques portray historical events or commemorate successful wars, while others are a vivid depiction of Benin court life and ritual. Several groups of plaques show clear stylistic similarities. William B. Fagg suggested that these plaques represent the work of master brass casters. Fagg, William, 1973, 'Nigerian Images', London: Lund Humphries Gunsch, Kathryn, 2018, 'Benin plaques: a 16th century imperial monument', London: Routledge, Taylor and Francis Group; Direct pair with BM plaque Af1898,0115.39. Oba carries flexible switch, uwenrhien otan, in preparation for sacrificial ceremony. His retainers shield him from the sun, and also from hostile spiritual forces.; Part of Processional Pillar Set 7. Row 3A. Oba with warriors holding shields aloft (Gunsch, 2018).; Plankensteiner 2007: These two plaques [with Af1898,0115.39] simultaneously arrived at the British Museum in 1898. Though a little different in size, iconographically and stylistically they are nearly identical. The corresponding features make it all the more likely that they were meant to be a pair. As is known about the old palace buildings at the time of these plaques' manufacturing, this sort of plaque was attached - and demonstrably in symmetrical ordering - onto the pillars of the verandas and entrances. The opulent jewelry of the central figure, identified as Oba Esigie by Barbara Blackmun (1997) based on a comparisson of depictions on carved ivories and plaques, comprises of a high beaded choker, a multirow necklace of agate and coral beads, large agate pendants hanging on the sides of the coral beaded cap, adorned with a feather, arm and foot rings, a precious wrapper, presumably made from imported Indian or Portuguese fabrics, and a luxurious belt-and hip decoration with a mask. In his right hand the king holds a ceremonial switch. His left hand rests on the hand of one of the two small and - except for some jewelry - naked attendants, who also carries a sword. The small attendant figure to the left of the plaque carries a spool-shaped 'ekpokin' box. The two larger figures wear precious clothes. They protect the Oba's head from both sides with raised shields. All attendant figures have an unusual hairdo: a hair lock adorns the left ear of the two shield carrying figures, the hairstyle of both small attendants are reminiscent of those worn by the Ooton priests and the hair crest of the Oduduwa masks. The depicted scene presumably represents a ritual at a palace ceremony, the Igue, for instance, or the Ugie Erha Oba.; This plaque shows an Oba surrounded by his attendants, two of whom are depicted holding up their shields in a formal protective stance. Only the Oba was allowed to be shaded in this way within the city. P. Girshick Ben-Amos, The art of Benin (London, The British Museum Press, 1995); Blurton, 1997 Brass was intimately associated with kingship in the Benin empire and plaques, cast by the lost-wax process, were used to cover the wooden beams that supported the roof in the royal palace, the centre of religious activities that controlled the well-being of the entire empire. Those seen here show scenes of court life and ritual which involve rulers, warriors and officials. Naked figures are royal pages, their nudity contrasting with the splendid cloth and coral-bead costumes of royalty. Depictions of the European adventurers and traders, who sold the metal from which the plaques were cast, also appear and local interest centred on their long hair, hooked noses, dress and weapons. Since they were seen as messengers of the god Olokun, a white-faced deity who sends wealth and children over the sea, their presence in the palace is probably less a celebration of their own wealth and power than those of the Oba (king), who is said to have defeated Olokun in battle, stripping him of his riches and finery.

description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Depicts single standing bearded figure, facing front, holding small bow in left hand. Wears high-fronted headdress with horsehair behind, leopard's tooth necklace, quadrangular bell on chest, body armour, armlets and wrap-around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges (largely missing). Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. One nail hole at centre left. Depicts three standing dignitaries, facing front, each holding eben sword aloft in right hand with left hand on belt. All wear deep beaded collar, leopard's tooth necklace, beaded sash across chest and beaded anklets. Body scarification on upper arms and torso. Figures in centre and on right wear bell-shaped beaded headgear with knob finial and feather at left side, multiple bracelets, wrap-around patterned skirts with leopard figures at left sides. Figure on right wears high domed helmet with feather at left, armlets, patterned wrap-around skirt.;

The two figures to the left are dressed very much like the central figure in Af1898,0115,51, the figure of a leopard being in each case visible just under the left arm. The third figure wears a conical cap, a waist-cloth imitating leopard-skin, and broad armlets. On the stomach of each are punctured marks, disposed herring-bone-wise, and on the upper arm a group of three lines. All have the usual long vertical cicatrices down the breast (Af1897,1224.2).

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Narrow plaque, rectangular in form without side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Crescent in top left and right corners. One nail hole at top left and one partial hole at top right. One hole at centre right. One hole at bottom left corner. Depicts standing Oba, facing forwards, holding two mudfish attached to waist belt. Oba wears cylindrical beaded crown with oro protrusion and three ornamental beads, deep beaded collar, protective king's bead on chest, tunic and patterned skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Three nail holes at top right, left and centre. Two holes at bottom right and centre. Depicts two standing warrior figures, facing front, holding spears in inner hands and shields in outer hands. Warriors have tiered hairstyles with large beads at side of forehead. Wear beaded necklaces, leopard's tooth necklaces, patterned wrap-around skirts and beaded anklets. Flanked on outside by smaller scale emada figures, bodies decorated throughout with geometric patterns, elongated marks down noses. Both emada have tiered hairstyles with plaits at both sides of face terminating in large beads. Wear beaded collars, beaded neckalces, and armlets. Hold circular fans in right hands.;

Sash tied on left hip, bell attached to left side, patterned wrap-around skirt.;

title: Benin Plaques
description: Relief plaque, lost-wax cast in brass. Wide plaque, rectangular in form with side flanges. Background surface decorated with river leaf patterns and stippling. Four nail holes along top, three holes at bottom, right, left and centre. Depicts warrior figure on right side, flanked by two courtiers, all facing front. Warrior holds hilt of sword in left hand. Has multiple hair plaits terminating in beads. Wears brimmed helmet, deep beaded collar, beaded sash across chest with multiple pendent strips and bells attached, beaded armlets and anklets, and fringed wrap-around skirt. Central courtier figure holds ekpokin container in left hand with right hand on top. Has hairstyle divided into short coils, multiple hair plaits at sides terminating in beads. Wears beaded choker, two beaded necklaces, armlets, sash tied over left hip and fringed wrap-around skirt. Courtier figure on far left holds unidentified object in left hand. Has similar hairstyle to other courtier. Wears single choker, armlets, sash tied over left hip and fringed wrap-around skirt.;
